title: Price and Inflation Expectations Survey (PIES) - 2026 Q1 Report
summary: Philadelphia Fed's Q1 2026 Price and Inflation Expectations Survey (PIES) shows Third District firms expect their own prices to rise 3.1% over the next four quarters, up from 2.6% in Q4 2025, while median U.S. inflation expectations held steady at 3.0% and mean expectations remained at 3.6%. Long-run median inflation expectations declined to 3.0% from 4.0%, though the mean rose to 7.4% from 6.0%, indicating significant dispersion in longer-term views.
key_points: Firms' mean forecast for own prices over the next four quarters rose to 3.1% in Q1 2026, up from 2.6% in Q4 2025
Median U.S. inflation expectation over the next four quarters was unchanged at 3.0% (lowest reading in over a year); mean remained at 3.6%
Reported own firm prices rose 2.8% over the past four quarters, down slightly from 3.0% last quarter
Expected compensation costs per employee: 3.2% over next four quarters, little changed from 3.3% last quarter and near pre-pandemic levels
Long-run (10-year) median inflation expectation declined to 3.0% from 4.0%, while mean rose to 7.4% from 6.0%, indicating wide dispersion in longer-term views
Manufacturing firms reported 4.0% own price growth (past year) versus 2.0% for nonmanufacturing; manufacturing expects 3.7% versus 2.5% for nonmanufacturing going forward
why_it_matters: This survey provides forward-looking inflation signals from the business sector. Rising own-price expectations suggest firms may be preparing to pass through higher costs, which could feed into broader inflation dynamics. Stable short-term U.S. inflation expectations (3.0% median) indicate firms see current price pressures as contained, but the widening gap between median and mean long-run expectations signals growing uncertainty that could influence Fed communication and rate path expectations.
macro_context: The survey indicates mixed signals for the inflation outlook: near-term U.S. inflation expectations are stable at levels still above the Fed's 2% target, while firms are pricing in higher own-price growth. Compensation expectations remain subdued near pre-pandemic levels, which may limit services inflation persistence. The divergence between median and mean long-run expectations (3.0% vs 7.4%) suggests significant tail risk concerns among some respondents.
notes: Published date (February 25, 2026) is inferred from the document header as PUBLISHED_AT metadata was empty. Survey responses collected February 9-19, 2026. Regional survey (Third District firms) - not nationally representative but methodologically rigorous with trimmed means. The large gap between median (3.0%) and mean (7.4%) for 10-year inflation expectations suggests some respondents hold extreme views; this dispersion itself is a notable finding. No balance of payments, monetary policy decisions, or market structure content present.
